opts: The defensemen were playing way too deep in their own zone and gave up the blue line too easily on the rush back
The forwards didn't transition back — when your team loses the puck, everyone turns around immediately. Staying in the offensive zone creates an odd-man rush against you.
The goalie should have come out to the top of the crease to challenge the rushers and cut down the angle on the shot
The coach should have called a timeout to reset the lines and get the forwards back into a proper defensive structure
The puck carrier was too slow crossing the blue line and should have sped up to get into the zone before the teammates
The two teammates entered the zone before the puck — the puck must cross the blue line first. Players without the puck need to time their entry and stay onside.
The ref made a bad call at the line and blew the whistle before the puck had actually crossed into the offensive zone
The blue line was too confusing for the skaters to read, so the forwards lost track of where the puck actually was
Stand still at the dot and watch what your defenseman does with the puck before making any kind of next move at all
Transition immediately to your assignment — if you won the draw, get to your position in the rush development. The faceoff result gives your team the advantage if everyone executes.
Skate to the bench for a quick line change because the puck is already safe with the defenseman and the shift is over now
Celebrate winning the draw with your linemates because winning a faceoff is a big deal and the team should enjoy the moment
Your deke wasn't smooth enough to beat the goalie and you needed more practice with stickhandling at full speed in close
You had an open shot and didn't take it — when you have a clear look at the net, shoot. Trying to be fancy when a simple shot works is how you waste chances.
You should have passed back to a teammate who had a better angle at the net instead of trying the deke yourself
The goalie was too good for the move and read it early so the puck got taken away before you could even finish the deke
The defenseman didn't skate fast enough behind the net to beat the forechecker before making the clearing play up the middle
They made a blind pass up the middle — under pressure, the safe play is the boards. Passing up the middle without looking is the most dangerous turnover in hockey.
The other players didn't call loudly enough for the puck so the defenseman had no choice but to just throw it up the middle
The goalie should have come out of the crease to play the puck behind the net for the D instead of staying in the blue paint
They should have skated faster through the neutral zone to beat the defenders with pure speed before they could close the gap
They held the puck too long — with three open teammates, making a pass early creates a much better play than trying to carry through traffic. Read and move the puck.
Their teammates weren't calling loudly enough for the puck so the puck carrier had no idea they were open for a pass across the ice
The defenders got lucky with the angle they took and just happened to close at the right moment to strip the puck away
The goalie should have seen the sneaky player behind the winger and yelled out a warning to cover the backdoor pass
The winger was puck watching instead of covering their player — in the D-zone, you need to know where your player is at all times, not just stare at the puck
The defenseman should have rotated over and helped cover the backdoor player even though it wasn't actually their assignment
The pass was too good and too hard to stop because the other team made a perfect play that nobody could have defended
The D tries to carry it out alone up the middle by skating through both forecheckers with quick stickhandling moves
D makes the safe play and passes to the winger on the wall — the middle is too risky with two forecheckers closing, and the boards are the safe route out
The D shoots it hard down the entire length of the ice to get the puck safely out and reset everything from a neutral position
The D freezes the puck against the boards behind the net to force a faceoff and get a quick shift change for the tired line
You pass to the corner to a teammate who can then cycle the puck down low and set up a much better scoring chance from below the goal line
You shoot through the screen — the goalie can't see the puck, so even a low shot through traffic has a great chance of going in or creating a rebound
You skate in closer to the net so you get a better angle, and then you shoot once you have a cleaner look past the screen players
You wait patiently for a better passing lane to open up so the screening teammates have a chance to reposition themselves in the slot
You give up on the play because the forward is gone and catching them from behind is pretty much impossible at this point anyway
You keep skating hard and try to get your stick in the shooting lane — even a stick in the lane can disrupt the shot or force a wider angle
You yell loudly at your defensemen for letting the attacker get behind them so they know not to make the same mistake again
You coast back slowly toward your own net because there's no chance of catching up and you want to save some energy for later
Give up the goal and just see what happens next because there's really nothing else you can do from flat on the ice like this
Use whatever you can reach — your stick, your glove, your skate — to get something in the way. Any part of you in the lane is better than nothing.
Stay completely still on the ice and hope the shooter somehow misses the wide-open net that you've given them with the mistake
Call for help from your defensemen so they can slide in front of the shooter and take away the shooting lane from where you're down
You forecheck hard even though you're tired because backing off now means the other team gets a clean, easy breakout up the ice
Your line changes — the puck is deep in their zone and they're behind their net, so this is the perfect time for a line change. Fresh legs coming on.
You stand still at the blue line and rest a few seconds to catch your breath before chasing the puck back down into the zone
You call timeout so your tired line can get a proper rest on the bench before the other team starts moving the puck up the ice
Wait patiently for the play to develop normally before you decide what to do with your positioning in the offensive zone right now
Drive hard to the net immediately — the opposing center just spent all their energy on the draw and the lane to the net may be briefly open
Skate all the way over to the corner and see what kind of play develops down low before you commit to any real position
Call for a pass from the defenseman at the point so you can get the puck back and try to set up a play in the high slot
They're being smart by waiting for the breakaway so they can score the moment the puck gets flipped up to them from the D-zone
They're cherry picking — not helping on defense. Everyone needs to defend when the puck is in your zone. Hanging back leaves your team short-handed.
The coach told them to stay there in case the puck came up the ice, so they're just following the game plan they were given
They're too tired to skate all the way back and help in the defensive zone, so they're just conserving their energy for later
Puck carrier keeps going hard up the ice alone and tries to beat both defenders with pure speed down the outside lane
Puck carrier sees the second defender cheat and makes the cross-ice pass to the wide-open forward for a high-quality scoring chance
Puck carrier dumps it into the zone so the forwards can chase and create a new play with an aggressive forecheck
Puck carrier stops and waits for the trailing forward to catch up before making any move into the offensive zone at all
Stand still and call for the puck loudly so the puck carrier knows exactly where you are and can make the pass to you
Skate into open space — cut to the net, move to a seam. Make yourself available AND drag your defender out of the puck carrier's area.
Go help the puck carrier in the battle for the puck by skating right into the pile to try to get the puck loose for the team
Skate out to the blue line right away so you're ready to make a breakout pass if the other team gets the puck back and clears
The teammate should have eyes in the back of their head to see the forechecker coming behind them even without turning to look around
Nobody communicated — someone should have yelled 'man on left!' to warn their teammate about the pressure coming. Communication prevents turnovers.
The forechecker was too fast to stop
The teammate should have passed the puck much sooner so they wouldn't have gotten caught behind the net with pressure coming in
Nothing really — just go out and defend hard
Their specific penalty kill assignments — who covers the point, who covers the half-wall, and what the plan is if they get the puck
To skate as fast as they can and chase every puck carrier around the zone without ever stopping until the penalty ends
To surround the puck carrier at all times with all four skaters so there's no chance for a clean pass or shot on the goalie
Pass immediately to the left winger because that's the first open option you see and quick passes are always the right play
Look at both defenders first — pass to whichever winger the defenders are leaving more open, or drive the middle if it's open
Shoot the puck from center ice right at the goalie to try to surprise them and maybe sneak one in through traffic
Dump the puck deep into the offensive zone and chase it down with all three forwards to start a forecheck right now
Your D shoots from the blue line immediately to try to sneak a quick one past the goalie before the defenders can react at all
Your D passes to a forward who's driving the net — their team is caught deep and your forwards should be attacking with speed
Your D passes it back to your goalie so the team can reset the breakout from behind the net with a calm, controlled play
Everyone stops and regroups in the neutral zone before pushing forward into the offensive zone as an organized unit together
Always cover the puck for a faceoff
Read the situation — if two opponents are about to get it, cover it immediately. If your team has it, leave it for them.
Always leave the puck for your team no matter what's happening in front of you because your team needs to start the breakout play
Kick the puck away from the crease as hard as you can to anywhere so the other team can't get a second shot on you at all
Push through your tiredness because the play needs you and your linemates are counting on you to come back on the backcheck
If you can't backcheck at full speed, get off and get a fresh player on. A tired player is worse than no player.
Slow jog back to the D-zone
Call out to the other team to stop their rush and give your team a chance to get set up in the defensive zone properly
Skate slowly and carefully across the crease to make sure you don't lose your balance or get caught out of position by the play
Push off the left post and use a strong T-push or shuffle across — keep your eyes on the puck the whole time
Turn around and skate quickly across
Stay on the left post a bit too long and hope the puck comes back to your side before you commit to moving across the crease
Stop the puck fully and then look around for a pass to a teammate in a better spot than the one you're currently in
One-time it low blocker side — the goalie hasn't moved yet and a one-touch in stride catches them off-balance
Stickhandle to slow yourself down and aim carefully at the open corner before you take the shot at the set goalie
Pass across to a winger on the other side who might have a better angle at the net than you do at this particular moment
Slow down because it's probably just going to come back out of their zone anyway so there's no point in really chasing it
Attack hard — a PK icing or clear can become a shorthanded chance if you pursue it aggressively. Speed wins that race.
Go straight to the bench for a quick line change since the clear is long enough to get fresh legs out onto the ice safely
Wait for your penalty kill teammate to come up the ice with you so you can pressure the puck together as a tandem up there
Nothing — ignore the shoulder and just play the puck straight up the way you would with any other shooter coming at you
They're likely shooting to your right — their body is opening to the left which usually means the shot goes
They're going to deke around you to the left
They'll pass
Never touch the puck because it's too risky and leaving the crease always gives the other team a chance to score on the empty net
Yes — if you can get there first and your winger is a step away, stop it for them so they have a clean pickup
Only if the other team definitely isn't coming for the puck too and you have a completely clear path behind your net to play it
Kick it with your skate to the corner
Go pressure the defenseman at the point who has the puck right now so they can't get off a clean shot through the screen
Move the screener out of the crease — your goalie can't see the shot and that's more dangerous than the shot itself
Stay in a passing lane so nothing gets through
Back up all the way to the crease yourself to help the goalie block any shot that might be coming from the point
Get out of the way fast
Tip the puck — even a slight redirection completely changes the trajectory and defeats the goalie's
Call for the shot to go wide
Skate away from the crease quickly
Wheel around the net and try to carry the puck out all by yourself straight through the pressure up the middle of the ice
Reverse the puck to the other side — one forechecker can't follow, buying time for your winger to get position
Rim it hard around the boards
Shoot it down the ice as far as you can for icing to get a whistle and a fresh faceoff in the defensive zone right now
Skate out of the crease to challenge the puck carrier and cut down their shooting angle before they can fire a shot at you
Stay in your crease, be ready for a shot, and watch for the pass — the D takes away the pass, you take the shot
Yell at your defenseman to do something
Come out past the crease to the top
Change lines immediately even if it causes confusion in the bench area because the tired players really do need a rest right away
Get your two best faceoff players and PK coverage players out — fresh legs and the right assignments matter more than speed
Stay out on the ice because the familiar lineup is better than rolling new guys out cold without any time to prepare or set
Send out the power play line instead because they have the best sticks on the team and can probably win the faceoff cleanly
Watch carefully to see if your goalie covers the rebound or plays the puck before you commit to going into the corner
Get to the rebound before the opposing player — or at least get your body position between them and the puck
Call loudly for your goalie to come out and play the puck in the corner before the opposing player can get to it first
Back up all the way to the crease
The tricky cross-ice pass — it leads to a faster breakout
The simple short pass to your D partner — take the safe option under pressure and let them move the puck from a better position
Try to carry it yourself around the back
Wait for a better option to appear because one of your teammates will probably get open soon if you just hold the puck
Watch the bodies carefully
Keep your eyes moving — track the puck through the traffic. Move with the puck, not with the players.
Wait patiently for the puck to come out of the pile into a clear area where you can see it and make a save
Drop immediately into your butterfly to cover the bottom of the net in case the puck squeaks out right at the goal line
Nothing at all — chasing pucks is actually a great PK strategy
Chasing breaks the box — gaps open up and the power play gets into the dangerous areas you were protecting
Chasing tires out the power play faster
Chasing makes the power play players hesitate and second-guess every pass they make around the outside of the box setup
You wait for the goalie to get fully set
You pass to a teammate in the slot
You shoot immediately at the open side — the goalie is out of position and you have the net to shoot at
You skate in a little closer first
Stay on the left side of the ice where the puck is and help your teammates battle for possession along the boards in the zone
Move immediately to the right side — the space is there and the D will have to follow you or leave you open
Go to the bench for a line change
Stand in front of the opposing net
Watch the puck carefully because it's way more important to know where the puck is than to know where your assigned man is
Watch your man — keep them in front of you so you know when they move. You can track the puck with your peripheral vision.
Watch both equally at once
Go get the puck yourself in the corner
Get to the puck first and try to skate it all the way out of the zone yourself before the opponent can close on you
Get your body between the opponent and the puck — inside position wins this battle even if you get there second
Poke at the puck with your stick from a distance and try to knock it loose without getting into the physical battle at all
Call loudly for your goalie to come out and get the puck while you block the opposing player from reaching the front of the net
Commit hard to the puck carrier on the left and try to take the puck off their stick before they can make any kind of play
Stay in the middle — take away the pass to the cutter and force the shot. Your goalie handles the shot.
Chase the cutter all the way to the right post to make sure they can't tap in any pass that comes across to the back door
Back up all the way to your own crease so you're right next to the goalie and can cover any rebound that comes out on a shot
Charge right at them and try to take the puck off their stick before they can get across the red line and into your zone
Back up at a pace that doesn't let them get speed on you, angle them toward the boards, and wait
Turn around and race them straight back to your net to try to beat them to the front before they can fire off a shot
Stand completely still at the red line and wait for them to come to you before you commit to any kind of defensive move
